headline: WASDE DAY: WHEAT RUNS 3%, HOGS REVERSE HARD
subheadline: USDA's July WASDE at 11 AM CT resets the supply picture as wheat posts its biggest session since spring and hogs erase yesterday's 11.8% drop in a single day.
lead: The WASDE dropped at 11 AM and the market answered fast. Wheat ran 18.5 cents to $6.30, corn added 8.5 cents to $4.38, and hogs posted a 15.7% session that erased yesterday's 11.8% break almost to the penny. That is not a coincidence: the USDA cut red meat production estimates and trimmed global wheat supply, and the funds that liquidated livestock yesterday came right back in this morning. The weekly thread question from Monday asked whether Crop Progress would confirm pollination-week crop health or build a weather premium into corn. Today the answer is partial: corn is firming on WASDE-driven supply tightening and heat dome positioning, but $4.38 is not yet a weather-premium price. The next leg needs the heat to verify in August crop condition data.
the_takeaway: WASDE cut supply on wheat and beef; the market priced it. Hold unpriced wheat if you can.
teaser: WASDE lit the complex; wheat ran 3%, hogs reversed 15%, and corn finally got off the mat.

one_number.unit: USDA 2026 projected US beef output, cut 150M lbs from June
one_number.context: USDA pulled 150 million pounds of beef production out of its 2026 estimate in today's WASDE, citing tight ready-to-market numbers driven partly by the ongoing Cargill Fort Morgan and Schuyler plant lockout that began May 19. That cut is the structural reason live cattle held $235.00 while feeders dipped today: the packer math is tightening, not loosening. Producers holding cattle into late summer have a USDA number now working in their favor.

yesterdays_call.summary: Yesterday's call: wheat up, toward $6.45, on the thesis that WASDE supply tightening would extend the move.
yesterdays_call.note: Wheat closed $6.30 today, up 18.5 cents and the biggest grain session since spring, but the $6.45 target was not reached. The call had the right direction and the right catalyst -- WASDE did tighten global wheat supply -- but the level was a step too far in a single session. Call did not play out; direction was right, target was not.

sections.title: WASDE Lights the Grain Complex
sections.body: Wheat led the session, gaining 18.5 cents to close at **$6.30**, the biggest move in the complex and the clearest WASDE print. The USDA tightened global wheat supply in the July report, and Feedstuffs flagged low production data as the headline driver. Corn added 8.5 cents to **$4.38** on nearby and 7.75 cents to **$4.60** on December, lifted by the same report and by heat dome positioning ahead of pollination. Beans came along but lagged: nearby closed **$11.97**, up 16.5 cents, while November only added 7 cents to **$11.89**. The old-crop/new-crop bean spread is telling you the market still has questions about August demand pace, even with the China $17 billion annual US ag purchase commitment through 2028 announced May 18 sitting on the board. Wheat is the section winner today, funds rotated in and the WASDE gave them a number to hang it on.
sections.catalyst: USDA July WASDE tightened global wheat supply; low production data flagged by Feedstuffs as primary driver.
sections.bottom_line: Wheat earned its 3% day. Corn along for the ride. Beans lagging new-crop tells you demand conviction is still partial.
sections.farmer_action: Unpriced wheat: $6.30 is the highest close since spring. If you have old-crop in storage with carry costs running, the WASDE just gave you a window. Scale sales above $6.30; next resistance is $6.45.
sections.vs_yesterday: Corn: reversed Thursday's 1.8% loss, WASDE provided the catalyst corn lacked all week.

sections.title: Hogs Reverse Hard, Cattle Split
sections.body: Lean hogs had their biggest single-day advance in recent memory, rising 15.7% to **$98.90**, almost perfectly erasing yesterday's 11.8% break. The catalyst is the USDA WASDE: red meat production estimates were cut, beef projected at 25.288 billion pounds (down 150 million from June) and pork directionally lower as well, and the funds that stepped out of hogs Thursday came right back in. This is not a new demand story; it is a supply-cut story, and that distinction matters for how long the bounce holds. Live cattle firmed 1.5% to **$235.00** while feeders dipped 0.5% to **$354.38**, a split that is consistent with processing-constrained dynamics from the ongoing Cargill Fort Morgan and Schuyler plant lockout, now in its eighth week with no resolution announced. The feeder softness into a live cattle rally is the lockout's fingerprint: feedlot demand is cautious when packing capacity is constrained.
sections.catalyst: USDA WASDE cut 2026 beef production estimate 150M lbs from June; Cargill lockout processing constraint persists.
sections.bottom_line: Hog reversal is supply math, not demand news. Cattle split remains the lockout's signature.
sections.vs_yesterday: Hogs: yesterday's 11.8% break fully reversed on WASDE supply cut; same funds, opposite direction.

sections.title: Hormuz Back on the Tape
sections.body: WTI crude settled at **$71.52**, off 1.2% on the session despite a headline from oilprice.com this morning that renewed US-Iran strikes have revived the Middle Eastern risk premium and lifted Brent above $76. The spread between Brent and WTI is doing the work here: US domestic supply, which the EIA confirmed is the world's largest for 2025, is creating a price ceiling on the domestic contract even as the Iran-Hormuz tensions, with the Strait of Hormuz premium that built since early April now deflating on diplomatic progress, show signs of reigniting. Kazakhstan extending its petroleum export ban six months as Hormuz tensions flare adds another supply-chain wrinkle. The practical input-cost read for US producers: diesel prices are not gaining with crude today because the domestic supply buffer is large, but the Hormuz story is not over and the USDA's own $500 million domestic fertilizer funding announcement signals the agency sees input-cost pressure as durable.
sections.catalyst: Renewed US-Iran strikes lift Brent above $76; Kazakhstan extends petroleum export ban six months; EIA confirms US as world's largest crude producer.
sections.bottom_line: Hormuz risk is back but domestic supply is cushioning the WTI price. Watch Brent/WTI spread for the real signal.

sections.title: Cattle Finishing Math Gets Harder
sections.body: The farmdoc daily analysis published this morning on cattle finishing breakeven prices is worth two minutes of your Friday. With live cattle at **$235.00** and feeders at **$354.38**, the feeder-to-live ratio sits at roughly 1.51, historically tight territory. USDA's average steer price projection of $251.10 built into today's WASDE implies the board is pricing a recovery from the current $235 level, but the Cargill lockout is removing roughly 2% of weekly US slaughter capacity, which means fed cattle are not clearing the system at the pace the price would imply. The benchmark milk drop of 94 cents noted in The Fence Post, with Class III at **$16.64** today, adds a second input-cost headwind for mixed operations: dairy and beef are both telling a supply-compression story, not a demand story.
sections.catalyst: USDA WASDE projects average steer price $251.10; farmdoc daily analysis on tight finishing margins; Class III milk at 52-week low proximity.
sections.bottom_line: Feeder/live ratio at 1.51 with packing capacity constrained is a margin squeeze, not a rally setup.

outside_the_pit.title: John Deere Right-to-Repair Settlement Draws Praise from Farm Groups
outside_the_pit.body: NFU and AFPI both applauded the Deere settlement today, with NFU President Rob Larew noting past promises from Deere have gone unfulfilled. If this settlement holds teeth, it is a structural input-cost story for the next decade: producers who can diagnose and repair their own equipment during planting and harvest windows without waiting for a dealer will see a real cost reduction that no futures price captures.

outside_the_pit.title: Drought Covers 75% of US Cowherd's Home Territory
outside_the_pit.body: A Fence Post report today puts 75% of the national cowherd in drought-affected areas, a number that complicates any herd-rebuild story. With the WASDE already cutting beef production on tight ready numbers, drought stress on cow-calf pairs means the 2027 supply picture could tighten further before it loosens. Producers in drought zones with bred heifers are carrying more risk than the current $235 live cattle price is pricing.

outside_the_pit.title: Pasture Mealybug Alert Issued Across Southeast US
outside_the_pit.body: Beef Magazine published an alert today on the pasture mealybug, an insect pest threatening pastures and hayfields across a wide swath of the Southeast. Coming on a day when Missouri is reporting strong hay yields after a delayed season, the contrast is sharp: the mid-South is building hay inventory while the Southeast faces a new pest pressure. Producers in affected states should consult extension services before the pest spreads into cool-season grass stands.

spread_to_watch.label: Nearby soybeans / November soybeans old-crop/new-crop
spread_to_watch.commentary: Nearby beans closed $11.97 against November at $11.89, an 8-cent old-crop premium that has been tightening all week. When old-crop commands a premium over new-crop, the market is saying current demand is tighter than fall supply expectations, which is the right read given China's purchase commitment and tight processing margins. If this spread flips to an inverse above 10 cents, that is the signal that old-crop demand is running ahead of the board's current pricing.

basis.headline: Wheat basis firming on WASDE; corn basis mixed east to west.
basis.body: **Wheat basis is tightening** at interior elevators following the WASDE supply cut, with merchandisers in the Central Plains reporting better-than-usual elevator interest on the close. Corn basis in the Eastern Belt is holding firm as ethanol grind continues to absorb old-crop supplies; the western Belt remains soft, consistent with the seasonal pattern and adequate pipeline stocks. Soybean basis is quietly firm at processors, though the old-crop/new-crop spread narrowing argues for producers to watch rather than chase the basis move aggressively into the weekend.

weekly_thread.question: Does today's Crop Progress print confirm pollination-week crop health, or add a weather premium that pulls corn back above $4.35?
weekly_thread.status_text: The week resolved on a WASDE catalyst rather than a Crop Progress catalyst: corn crossed back above $4.35 on supply tightening and heat dome positioning, not a crop condition break. Pollination-week risk is still live, but today's $4.38 close is a WASDE price, not a weather-premium price; those are two different stories and only one of them has legs into August.

the_more_you_know.title: The Old-Crop/New-Crop Spread Is the Market's Honest Opinion About Fall
the_more_you_know.body: Today's 8-cent premium of nearby soybeans over November tells you something the closing price alone doesn't: the market trusts current demand more than it trusts fall supply. When old-crop trades above new-crop, commercials and end-users are paying up for beans they need now rather than locking in fall bushels at a discount. That spread widened this week even as the China $17 billion annual US ag purchase commitment sits on the board and November beans only gained 7 cents today versus 16.5 for nearby. The practical read for producers: if you have old-crop beans in the bin, the market is currently paying you a carry premium to deliver them now. If the spread flips to an inverse wider than 10 cents, old-crop demand is running hot enough that you should be selling bushels, not storing them. Watch it weekly, not daily.

watch_list.desc: USDA Crop Progress: corn condition rating above 65% good/excellent holds the weather-premium lid; below 60% and the pollination story adds real cents to December corn above $4.60.
Weekly export sales: beans above 400K MT keeps the old-crop/new-crop spread from inverting; below 300K MT and the nearby premium fades fast.
Cargill Fort Morgan and Schuyler lockout: any resolution announcement closes the feeder/live cattle spread; no resolution keeps the 1.51 ratio under pressure into August.

Brent/WTI spread: Brent above $76 with WTI at $71.52 is a $4.48 gap. If that widens past $6, US diesel prices start following Brent higher regardless of domestic supply. Watch it before your next fuel contract renewal.
Hormuz shipping lane traffic: five tankers cleared the week of May 25 on diplomatic progress; any confirmed slowdown in transit numbers is the leading indicator for crude above $75 WTI.

daily_quote.text: No winter lasts forever; no spring skips its turn.

source_summary: USDA July 2026 WASDE (11:00 AM CT release); Brownfield Ag News closing futures; Feedstuffs afternoon recap; oilprice.com Hormuz/Kazakhstan reports; farmdoc daily cattle finishing analysis; The Fence Post drought and milk benchmark reports; Beef Magazine pasture mealybug alert; EIA crude production data; farmpolicynews.illinois.edu USDA fertilizer funding.
